front: Figure: escaped slavery to become the leading Black abolitionist orator, writer, and statesman of 19th-century America; founded and edited the newspaper The North Star and wrote autobiographies including My Bondage and My Freedom
Figure: regarded as the most photographed American of the 19th century
Figure: as a child, was given a supposed magical root by a man named Sandy, meant to ward off harm if kept on the right side of his body
Figure: as a child, befriended a boy named Daniel and tricked older white boys into giving him reading lessons by pretending to already know more than them
Figure: recalled witnessing the overseer Mr. Gore shoot the enslaved man Demby, who had refused to leave a stream
Figure: learned to read partly thanks to Sophia Auld, before her husband Hugh Auld forbade it, and was later transformed after reading a copy of The Columbian Orator
Figure: physically fought and defeated this sadistic 'slave-breaker,' to whom he had been leased, in a climactic two-hour fight in a horse stable that permanently ended the man's abuse of him
Figure: worked as a caulker at a Baltimore shipyard while enslaved to Hugh and Thomas Auld, forced to turn over all his wages
Figure: married this woman shortly after escaping from Maryland in 1838, before settling with her in New Bedford, Massachusetts
Figure: after escaping to New Bedford, Massachusetts in 1838, was given food by this man
Document: published in 1845 while its author lived in Lynn, Massachusetts, with a foreword by William Lloyd Garrison
back: Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass, an American Slave

front: Document: this 1855 expanded rewrite of the 1845 Narrative added a new character, Caroline, who milks a cow, to the fight scene with Edward Covey
Document: before recounting his fight with Edward Covey, its narrator described ships sailing by that seemed like 'shrouded ghosts,' 'robed in purest white'
back: Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass, an American Slave

front: Institution: founded and edited this Rochester, New York-based abolitionist newspaper beginning in 1847; its office was located near that of fellow narrative-writer Harriet Jacobs
Figure: saw photography as a counterpoint to minstrelsy, which he frequently denounced in essays for The North Star, which he edited from 1847 to 1851

Document: delivered before the Rochester Ladies' Anti-Slavery Sewing Society in 1852, this speech declares 'your shout of liberty and equality' a 'hollow mockery' and states 'it is not light that is needed, but fire'
back: "What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?"

front: Document: this Ben Franklin-inspired lecture, delivered repeatedly for the Redpath Lyceum Bureau, states 'I do not think much of the good luck theory'
Document: his last major speech, this address lists 'three distinct periods of persecution of negroes'
Document: this shorter 1841 speech recounts anecdotes of racism its speaker witnessed in Christian services in both the North and South
Figure: in one speech, grounded morality and social progress in humanity's appreciation of art, calling humanity a 'picture-making animal'
Figure: claimed men had rights to 'the ballot box, jury box, and cartridge box' in a speech about the 'four boxes of liberty'
Figure: deliberately eschewed props and controlled his gaze and orientation toward the camera in his photographs so as not to project the image of a 'happy slave'
Figure: was photographed several times by John White Hearn, who had earlier helped him avoid arrest for his near-participation in John Brown's raid on Harpers Ferry
Figure: gave lectures on photography titled 'Age of Pictures,' 'Life Pictures,' and 'Pictures and Progress'; many of his photographs were preserved in albums collected by Arabella Chapman
Figure: attended the 1848 Seneca Falls Convention, convened by Elizabeth Cady Stanton and Lucretia Mott to discuss women's rights
Figure: refused to join John Brown's 1859 raid on Harpers Ferry after a meeting at a Chambersburg quarry, believing the plan too risky, and later publicly opposed it as counterproductive
Figure: this close collaborator, with whom Douglass may have had a romance, organized a sewing society to promote his work
Figure: this German-born intellectual, who sought to translate one of Douglass's works, called his wife Anna Murray a 'veritable beast' and introduced him to German idealist thought

Figure: criticized this fellow escaped slave's eager public retelling of his own dramatic escape story, in which he shipped himself in a crate, believing it would worsen the chances of future escapees

Figure: this escaped-slave novelist, author of Clotel, the first novel published by an African American, was considered a rival; his own slave narrative was said to be second in popularity only to Douglass's
Institution: two of Douglass's sons served in this Black Civil War regiment, led by Robert Gould Shaw in the assault on Fort Wagner
Institution: this Reconstruction-era newspaper was founded by Frederick Douglass
Institution: Frederick Douglass served as president of this Reconstruction-era financial institution

Figure: served as U.S. Minister Resident and Consul General to Haiti and was removed from the post after a meeting broke down with the Haitian official Antenor Firmin during the 'Mole Saint-Nicolas Affair'
Figure: ran as Victoria Woodhull's vice-presidential running mate on the Equal Rights Party ticket in the 1872 election, without having been consulted beforehand
Figure: his Rochester house burned down while he was running for the vice presidency, prompting his eventual move to this Washington, D.C. estate
Figure: gave a speech marking the fourteenth anniversary of Storer College, citing the actions that led John Brown's men to North Elba, New York, where 'the dust now settles amongst the Adirondacks'
Poem: imagines its subject remembered not with 'legends or poems' or 'bronze' but 'with the lives grown out of his life'
Figure: the author Charles Chesnutt wrote a biography of this abolitionist
Figure: David W. Blight's 2018 biography of this man calls him a 'Prophet' of Freedom
Work: John Stauffer co-authored this illustrated biography examining its subject as the most photographed American of the 19th century
Work: this Paul Gilroy book reads Douglass's fight with Covey as an 'ideal speech situation' in a chapter on the 'Antinomies of Modernity'
Novel: in this John Updike novel, the character Rabbit reads aloud the account of Douglass's fight with Covey while another character reacts on the couch
